schools.summary: Gill: Joseph could not refrain himself, could not much longer keep from tears, such an effect Judah's speech had on his passions; he bade every man go out that none might witness the discovery; the witnesses note Joseph, no longer able to refrain, bidding every man go out, that none stood with him while he made himself known to his brethren — the overcoming of Joseph's restraint, and his clearing the room for the tender revelation (the dammed-up love breaking forth at last: 'Joseph could not refrain himself... and he cried, Cause every man to go out from me. And there stood no man with him, while Joseph made himself known unto his brethren'; the breaking-point, that Judah's moving plea so wrought upon Joseph that he 'could not refrain himself' any longer, the long disguise giving way; his tender care in clearing the room, 'Cause every man to go out from me,' that he sent away all the Egyptians, that no stranger should witness the brothers' shame in the discovery nor the sacred and tearful scene of reconciliation; that he would make himself known 'unto his brethren' alone, sparing them and honouring the moment; the type of Christ the commentators draw, who reveals himself to his own in private love).

debates.question: Why does Joseph clear the room before revealing himself?

sides.position: In tender care, that no Egyptian stranger should witness the brothers' shame in the discovery, nor the sacred and tearful scene of reconciliation — making himself known to his brethren alone

themes: Joseph could not refrain himself; and he cried, Cause every man to go out from me
while Joseph made himself known unto his brethren
Joseph's restraint overcome, clearing the room of all strangers
a type of Christ revealing himself to his own in private love, sparing their shame

schools.summary: Gill: he gave forth his voice in weeping; having put such a violent restraint on himself, the flood of tears was the greater, and his voice the stronger and louder; the witnesses note Joseph weeping aloud, his voice so loud that the Egyptians and the house of Pharaoh heard — the bursting forth of Joseph's long-pent grief and love in loud weeping (the restrained feeling pouring out in a flood: 'he wept aloud: and the Egyptians and the house of Pharaoh heard'; the force of it, that, having so long and so hard restrained himself, weeping before only in secret, Joseph now 'wept aloud,' gave full and loud voice to his weeping, the long-dammed flood breaking out the stronger for being held back; the loudness, that though the Egyptians were sent from the room, his weeping was so great that 'the Egyptians and the house of Pharaoh heard' it even from without, and the report reached the palace; the depth of feeling in this, the tenderness of a heart that had borne so much, now overflowing in love and joy at the revealing; that such weeping was no weakness but the fulness of a forgiving and longing heart).

themes: And he wept aloud
and the Egyptians and the house of Pharaoh heard
Joseph's long-restrained feeling bursting forth in loud weeping
the dammed flood breaking out the stronger, heard even in Pharaoh's house

schools.summary: Gill: as soon as he could compose himself and utter his words, the first thing he said was that he was Joseph, his right and Hebrew name; the witnesses note Joseph saying, 'I am Joseph; doth my father yet live?' and his brethren, troubled at his presence, unable to answer — the astonishing self-disclosure and the brothers' speechless dread (the great word falling upon them: 'I am Joseph; doth my father yet live? And his brethren could not answer him; for they were troubled at his presence'; the two things in Joseph's word, first the staggering revelation, 'I am Joseph,' the brother they had sold, the one they thought dead or lost, now the lord of Egypt before them, and second the tender, longing question of a son, 'doth my father yet live?' the thing nearest his heart; the brothers' terror, 'his brethren could not answer him; for they were troubled at his presence,' that they were struck dumb with amazement and fear, their old guilt rising up before them, dreading what this brother, now in power, might do to them; the type of Christ the commentators mark, that as the brethren were confounded before the Joseph they had wronged, so will the guilty be before Christ revealed).

themes: I am Joseph; doth my father yet live?
his brethren could not answer him; for they were troubled at his presence
the staggering revelation and the son's longing question
the brothers struck dumb with dread, their old guilt rising, as the guilty before Christ revealed

schools.summary: Gill: very probably Joseph sat in a chair of state, and through reverence of him they kept at a distance, or being filled with shame and fear they drew back; he kindly bade them come near; the witnesses note Joseph bidding his brethren come near, and saying, 'I am Joseph your brother, whom ye sold into Egypt' — the gracious drawing near, Joseph owning the trembling men as his brothers even while naming their sin (Joseph stooping to reassure them: 'Come near to me, I pray you. And they came near. And he said, I am Joseph your brother, whom ye sold into Egypt'; the tenderness of 'Come near to me, I pray you,' that, seeing them draw back in fear and shame, Joseph kindly calls them close, not as a judge but as a brother, that they might know his love; the weight of 'I am Joseph your brother,' that he owns them still as 'brother,' not casting them off for their crime; the plain naming of the sin, 'whom ye sold into Egypt,' not to upbraid but that they might know he fully knew what they had done and yet forgave; that this gentle drawing near and owning of them is a tender picture of grace, knowing the worst yet receiving the guilty).

themes: Come near to me, I pray you. And they came near
I am Joseph your brother, whom ye sold into Egypt
Joseph kindly calling the frightened brethren near, owning them still as brothers
naming their sin not to upbraid but to show he knew the worst and forgave

schools.summary: Gill: they ought to be grieved for their sin and show godly sorrow and true repentance, but not to excess so as to be swallowed up with overmuch sorrow; for God had overruled their evil to a saving end; the witnesses note Joseph bidding them not be grieved nor angry with themselves for selling him, for God had sent him before them to preserve life — the great word of comfort and the first statement of the doctrine of providence (Joseph lifting their crushing guilt by pointing to God: 'be not grieved, nor angry with yourselves, that ye sold me hither: for God did send me before you to preserve life'; the comfort, 'be not grieved, nor angry with yourselves,' that Joseph, far from avenging, seeks to ease the anguish of their conscience, not that their sin was small, they ought to repent, but that they should not be 'swallowed up with overmuch sorrow'; the deep truth he sets against their guilt, 'for God did send me before you to preserve life,' that, while they had wickedly 'sold' him, God had overruled their evil deed to a saving end, sending Joseph ahead into Egypt to preserve many alive in the famine; the great doctrine of providence here, that God works his good purpose even through the sins of men without excusing the sin; the grace of Joseph, seeing God's hand above his brothers' malice).

debates.question: How does Joseph reconcile his brothers' sin with God's hand in his coming to Egypt?

sides.position: By the doctrine of providence — though they wickedly sold him, God overruled their evil deed to a saving end ('God did send me before you to preserve life'); God works his good purpose through men's sins without excusing the sin (cf. Genesis 50:20)

themes: be not grieved, nor angry with yourselves, that ye sold me hither
for God did send me before you to preserve life
Joseph easing their guilt by the doctrine of providence
God overruling their evil deed to a saving end, without excusing the sin

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: 'earing' means ploughing or tilling the ground, from the old English to ear, to plough, not to do with the ears of corn; the witnesses note Joseph saying the famine had been two years and five more remained, in which there should be neither ploughing nor harvest — the reason of God's providence laid out, the long famine that made Joseph's mission needful (Joseph showing the greatness of the deliverance by the length of the want: 'these two years hath the famine been in the land: and yet there are five years, in the which there shall neither be earing nor harvest'; the account, that the famine had already lasted 'two years,' and that 'five years' more were yet to come, seven in all, in which 'there shall neither be earing nor harvest'; Clarke's note on 'earing,' that it is the old English word for ploughing or tilling, not the ears of corn, so that for five years there should be no ploughing and no reaping, the land yielding nothing; that this long and total famine showed why God had sent Joseph before, for without the stored corn the whole region must have perished; that Joseph, knowing the future by God's revealing, sets the case plainly, that his brethren might see the wisdom of God's providence).

themes: these two years hath the famine been in the land
yet there are five years, in the which there shall neither be earing nor harvest
Joseph laying out the long famine that made his mission needful
showing why God had sent him before, lest the whole region perish

schools.summary: Gill: he repeats this to impress on his brethren a sense of the good providence of God in bringing him to Egypt before them, to make provision for their future welfare; the witnesses note Joseph saying again that God sent him before them to preserve a posterity and to save their lives by a great deliverance — the second and fuller statement of providence, God's saving purpose for the covenant family (Joseph pressing home the gracious design of God: 'God sent me before you to preserve you a posterity in the earth, and to save your lives by a great deliverance'; the repetition, 'God sent me before you,' twice pressed, that his brethren might be fully assured it was God's doing, the good providence above their evil; the high purpose named, 'to preserve you a posterity in the earth,' that God's design was to keep alive the chosen seed, the family of Jacob, from whom the promised nation and the Messiah should come, that they should not perish in the famine; the greatness of the salvation, 'to save your lives by a great deliverance,' no small or common rescue but a great and wonderful preservation of the covenant people; that Joseph sees the whole, his own suffering and rise, as God's means to save the church in seed).

themes: God sent me before you to preserve you a posterity in the earth
and to save your lives by a great deliverance
Joseph pressing home God's saving purpose for the covenant family
the chosen seed of Jacob kept alive, from whom the nation and Messiah should come

schools.summary: Gill: this is not to be understood absolutely, as if they had no concern at all in sending him thither, they sold him to the Ishmaelites, but God overruled it; the witnesses note Joseph saying it was not they but God that sent him, who made him a father to Pharaoh, lord of all his house, and ruler over all Egypt — the supreme statement of providence and the height of Joseph's exaltation (Joseph speaking the deepest word of all: 'it was not you that sent me hither, but God: and he hath made me a father to Pharaoh, and lord of all his house, and a ruler throughout all the land of Egypt'; the meaning of 'not you... but God,' that it is not absolute, the brothers truly sinned in selling him, but means that God's hand and purpose were so far above theirs that the deed was, in its great design, God's sending not theirs; the careful balance the commentators keep, that the brothers' guilt is not denied, they did sell him, yet God's overruling sovereignty is the higher and governing cause; the height of Joseph's place, 'a father to Pharaoh,' his honoured counsellor and provider, 'lord of all his house,' and 'a ruler throughout all the land of Egypt,' the very dominion his dreams had foreshown; that Joseph traces all his exaltation to God's hand).

debates.question: How can Joseph say 'not you... but God' sent him, when the brothers did sell him?

sides.position: Not absolutely — the brothers truly sinned in selling him, but God's overruling sovereignty and purpose were so far above theirs that the deed was, in its great design, God's sending; the human guilt is not denied, yet God's hand is the higher governing cause

themes: it was not you that sent me hither, but God
a father to Pharaoh, and lord of all his house, and a ruler throughout all the land of Egypt
the supreme statement of providence over the brothers' guilt
the height of Joseph's exaltation, the dominion his dreams foreshowed

schools.summary: Gill: Joseph was desirous his father should know as soon as possible that he was alive, and in what circumstances; Canaan lay higher than Egypt, hence 'go up'; the witnesses note Joseph bidding them haste to his father with the message that God had made him lord of Egypt, and to come down without delay — the eager longing of Joseph to bring his father to him, and his message of honour ascribed to God (Joseph's heart pressing toward his father: 'Haste ye, and go up to my father, and say... God hath made me lord of all Egypt: come down unto me, tarry not'; the urgency, 'Haste ye... come down unto me, tarry not,' that Joseph, longing to see his father after twenty-two years, would have no delay but bids them hasten with the news; the message, 'Thus saith thy son Joseph, God hath made me lord of all Egypt,' that Joseph would have his father know both that he lived and that God had raised him to greatness, ascribing his honour to God; the tenderness in 'thy son Joseph,' the long-lost son sending word to the aged father who mourned him as dead; the providence drawing Jacob's whole house to Egypt, as God had purposed).

themes: Haste ye, and go up to my father... Thus saith thy son Joseph, God hath made me lord of all Egypt
come down unto me, tarry not
Joseph's eager longing to bring his father down to him
the long-lost son's message of honour ascribed to God, to the father who mourned him

schools.summary: Gill: Goshen was that part of Egypt which bordered on Arabia, a land fit for flocks and herds; the witnesses note Joseph appointing that his father should dwell in Goshen, near to him, with his children, grandchildren, flocks, herds, and all he had — the loving provision of a place near himself for the whole house of Jacob (Joseph planning for them a home in the best of the land: 'thou shalt dwell in the land of Goshen, and thou shalt be near unto me, thou, and thy children, and thy children's children, and thy flocks, and thy herds, and all that thou hast'; the choice of Goshen, the part of Egypt bordering Arabia, a rich pasture-land 'fit for flocks and herds,' and so suited to Jacob's house of shepherds; the nearness, 'thou shalt be near unto me,' that Joseph would have his father close, that he might see him often and provide for him; the breadth of the welcome, 'thou, and thy children, and thy children's children, and thy flocks, and thy herds, and all that thou hast,' the whole household, every soul and all their substance, brought down and settled; the providence, that Goshen, a land apart, would keep Israel separate and let them grow into a nation).

themes: thou shalt dwell in the land of Goshen, and thou shalt be near unto me
thou, and thy children, and thy children's children, and thy flocks, and thy herds, and all that thou hast
Joseph appointing Goshen, near himself, for his father's whole house
a rich pasture fit for shepherds, a land apart where Israel would grow into a nation

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: the famine having lasted only two years and Jacob having preserved his flocks, the family was not yet utterly impoverished but would be without help; the witnesses note Joseph promising to nourish his father and household through the five years of famine, lest they come to poverty — the assurance of full provision, the lord of Egypt undertaking to keep his family (Joseph pledging all that they need: 'there will I nourish thee; for yet there are five years of famine; lest thou, and thy household, and all that thou hast, come to poverty'; the promise, 'there will I nourish thee,' that in Goshen Joseph himself would provide for his father and all his house, sparing them want; the reason, 'for yet there are five years of famine,' that the worst was not over, five hard years yet remaining, in which without help the family must waste away; Ellicott's note that, the famine being yet but two years old and Jacob having kept his flocks, they were not utterly ruined but would soon 'come to poverty' if not nourished; the care of Joseph, the great ruler stooping to feed his own from the stores of Egypt; the providence, that Joseph's high place was given that he might thus save his father's house).

themes: there will I nourish thee; for yet there are five years of famine
lest thou, and thy household, and all that thou hast, come to poverty
Joseph pledging to nourish his father's house through the famine
the lord of Egypt stooping to feed his own, his high place given to save his house

schools.summary: Gill: they were eyewitnesses of his being alive, having seen him, and even Benjamin, who could not be suspected of any design; the witnesses note Joseph appealing to their eyes, and Benjamin's, that it was his own mouth speaking to them — the proof of his identity set before their very eyes, that they might believe (Joseph calling them to be sure of the wonder: 'your eyes see, and the eyes of my brother Benjamin, that it is my mouth that speaketh unto you'; the appeal to sight, 'your eyes see... that it is my mouth that speaketh,' that they should not doubt so amazing a thing but be assured by their own eyes that the man before them was truly Joseph, alive and speaking; the joining of 'the eyes of my brother Benjamin,' his full brother, who could be no party to any deceit, as a further and trusted witness; that Joseph speaks, very likely, in their own Hebrew tongue, 'my mouth that speaketh,' which the governor would not have known, a further proof; the tenderness in naming 'Benjamin' his brother, drawing his own near in the proof).

themes: your eyes see, and the eyes of my brother Benjamin
that it is my mouth that speaketh unto you
Joseph's proof of his identity to their very eyes
very likely speaking in their own Hebrew tongue, which the governor would not have known

schools.summary: Gill: they were to tell his father of all his glory in Egypt, his wealth and riches, his grandeur and dignity, his power and authority; the witnesses note Joseph bidding them tell his father of all his glory in Egypt and all they had seen, and to haste and bring him down — the charge to report his greatness to Jacob and to hasten the old man's coming (Joseph would have his father know the whole: 'ye shall tell my father of all my glory in Egypt, and of all that ye have seen; and ye shall haste and bring down my father hither'; the message, 'tell my father of all my glory in Egypt,' his wealth and riches, his grandeur and dignity, his power and authority, that Jacob might know to what height God had raised his lost son; that Joseph would have them tell 'all that ye have seen,' the full account of his honour, that the report might be sure and the aged father persuaded; the renewed urgency, 'ye shall haste and bring down my father hither,' that, above all his glory, Joseph longed for his father's coming and would have no delay; the love of Joseph, that his greatness was sweet to him chiefly as it could serve and honour his father).

themes: ye shall tell my father of all my glory in Egypt, and of all that ye have seen
ye shall haste and bring down my father hither
Joseph charging them to report his glory and hasten Jacob's coming
his greatness sweet to him chiefly as it could serve and honour his father

schools.summary: Gill: he fell upon his neck first because Benjamin was his own brother by father and mother's side, and wept over him for joy that he had found him; the witnesses note Joseph falling on Benjamin's neck and weeping, and Benjamin weeping on his neck — the tender embrace of the two full brothers, their tears of joy at meeting (love overflowing between the sons of Rachel: 'he fell upon his brother Benjamin's neck, and wept; and Benjamin wept upon his neck'; that Joseph 'fell upon his brother Benjamin's neck' first of all the brethren, because Benjamin was 'his own brother by father and mother's side,' the only other child of his mother Rachel, dearest to him of all; the mutual joy, 'and Benjamin wept upon his neck,' that the two brothers wept together in each other's arms, tears not of grief now but of joy, the long parting ended; the depth of natural affection here, sanctified and sweet, the two who had been so long divided now joined again; that the tears of the brothers tell what words could not, the fulness of love restored).

themes: he fell upon his brother Benjamin's neck, and wept
and Benjamin wept upon his neck
the joyful embrace of the two full sons of Rachel
their tears now of joy not grief, the long parting ended

schools.summary: Gill: he kissed all his brethren in their turns, to testify his real affection for them and hearty reconciliation to them; the witnesses note Joseph kissing all his brethren and weeping over them, after which they talked with him — the full reconciliation sealed with a kiss, the brothers' fear giving way to free converse (Joseph's love embracing not Benjamin only but all: 'he kissed all his brethren, and wept upon them: and after that his brethren talked with him'; the breadth of his love, 'he kissed all his brethren,' not Benjamin alone but every one, even those who had sold him, 'in their turns,' the kiss the token of full forgiveness and 'hearty reconciliation'; the weeping, 'and wept upon them,' the tears of a reconciled heart, washing away the old wrong; the happy result, 'after that his brethren talked with him,' that, their fear now melted by his love and tears, the brothers found tongue at last and spoke freely with him, the dread turned to glad converse; the picture of forgiveness completing reconciliation, the offended one winning back the offenders by love).

themes: he kissed all his brethren, and wept upon them
and after that his brethren talked with him
Joseph kissing and weeping over all, even those who sold him, in token of full forgiveness
their fear melting into free converse, the offended one winning back the offenders by love

schools.summary: Gill: the report was carried to court and there told by some who had overheard, that Joseph's brethren were come, and it pleased Pharaoh and his servants; the witnesses note the news of Joseph's brethren reaching Pharaoh's house, and Pharaoh and his servants being well pleased — the king's glad welcome of the tidings, the favour shown to Joseph extended to his kindred (the report spreading and welcomed: 'the fame thereof was heard in Pharaoh's house, saying, Joseph's brethren are come: and it pleased Pharaoh well, and his servants'; that the news of the brothers' coming reached 'Pharaoh's house,' carried thither by those who had heard Joseph's loud weeping; the gladness, 'it pleased Pharaoh well, and his servants,' that the king and his court rejoiced at the tidings, both out of regard for Joseph, whom they so honoured, and gladly to welcome his family; the providence in this favour, that God so inclined the heart of Pharaoh that the coming of the Hebrew shepherds was not grudged but welcomed; the honour reflected on Joseph, that the king's love for him reached even to his unknown brethren).

themes: the fame thereof was heard in Pharaoh's house, saying, Joseph's brethren are come
and it pleased Pharaoh well, and his servants
Pharaoh's glad welcome of the tidings, the favour to Joseph reaching his kindred
God inclining the king's heart so the Hebrew shepherds were welcomed, not grudged

schools.summary: Gill: Joseph, it is highly probable, waited upon Pharaoh to acquaint him with the coming of his brethren, and Pharaoh gave these directions; the witnesses note Pharaoh bidding Joseph tell his brethren to lade their beasts and go to Canaan — the king's own command joining with Joseph's wish to bring down the family (Pharaoh himself ordering their return for their father: 'Say unto thy brethren, This do ye; lade your beasts, and go, get you unto the land of Canaan'; that Joseph, in honour, had laid the matter before Pharaoh, and the king now adds his own royal will to Joseph's, bidding the brothers 'lade your beasts' with corn and 'go... unto the land of Canaan' to fetch their father and households; the graciousness of the king, that he not only permits but commands and furthers the coming of the Hebrews, taking the matter up as his own; that Pharaoh's command made the way plain and honourable, the brothers coming not as mere strangers but at the king's bidding; the providence, that God moved the heart of the greatest monarch of the earth to forward the settling of Israel in Egypt).

themes: Pharaoh said unto Joseph, Say unto thy brethren, This do ye; lade your beasts, and go
get you unto the land of Canaan
Pharaoh adding his royal command to Joseph's wish
God moving the greatest monarch to forward the settling of Israel in Egypt

schools.summary: Gill: they were all to be brought, father and households, families, for they were all married persons with children, and no doubt servants also; the witnesses note Pharaoh bidding them bring their father and households and promising them the good of the land and the fat of Egypt — the king's generous promise of the best of Egypt to Joseph's kindred (Pharaoh offering them abundance: 'take your father and your households, and come unto me: and I will give you the good of the land of Egypt, and ye shall eat the fat of the land'; the breadth of the welcome, 'take your father and your households,' the whole family, every soul, the aged Jacob and all the married sons with their wives, children, and servants, to 'come unto me'; the king's bounty, 'I will give you the good of the land of Egypt, and ye shall eat the fat of the land,' that Pharaoh promises them not a bare refuge but the best and richest of all Egypt, the choicest produce and the fattest portions; the favour of God in this, that the famine-stricken family of Jacob should be invited to 'eat the fat of the land' of plenty; the honour put on Joseph, whose worth so commended his kindred to the king).

themes: take your father and your households, and come unto me
and ye shall eat the fat of the land
Pharaoh's generous promise of the good and fat of Egypt
not a bare refuge but the best and richest, the favour of God on Joseph's kindred

schools.summary: Gill: Joseph had his orders from Pharaoh, full power and authority to do these things; he was commanded to send wagons; the witnesses note Pharaoh commanding that wagons be taken out of Egypt for the little ones and wives, and to bring their father — the king's thoughtful provision of wagons for the weak and the aged (Pharaoh seeing even to the comfort of the journey: 'take you wagons out of the land of Egypt for your little ones, and for your wives, and bring your father, and come'; the care for the weak, 'wagons... for your little ones, and for your wives,' that Pharaoh, foreseeing the long journey would be hard for the children and the women, ordered 'wagons,' carts, then a rare and royal provision, to carry them in ease; the special thought for 'your father,' the aged Jacob, who could not well travel the long way on foot or beast but should be brought in comfort; that Joseph had the king's full warrant for all this, 'thou art commanded,' acting with royal authority; the kindness of God moving the king to so tender a care for the family of his servant).

themes: take you wagons out of the land of Egypt for your little ones, and for your wives
and bring your father, and come
Pharaoh's thoughtful provision of wagons for the weak and the aged Jacob
the kindness of God moving the king to so tender a care for his servant's family

schools.summary: Gill: 'regard not your stuff,' your vessels, utensils, household goods; he would not have them concerned if they could not bring all their goods; the witnesses note Pharaoh bidding them not regard their household stuff, for the good of all Egypt was theirs — the king's bounty that made the family's old goods not worth the carrying (Pharaoh outdoing all their possessions: 'regard not your stuff; for the good of all the land of Egypt is yours'; the meaning of 'regard not your stuff,' that they need not trouble to bring their 'vessels, utensils, household goods,' nor grieve to leave behind what they could not carry, the long journey making it hard to bring all; the reason, 'for the good of all the land of Egypt is yours,' that Pharaoh would furnish them anew with the best of Egypt, so that their old poor stuff in famine-struck Canaan was not worth the bringing; the greatness of the king's generosity, freely opening the riches of Egypt to the family of Joseph; the providence and grace of God, that the poor pilgrims should be so royally provided, a token of how God provides for his own).

themes: Also regard not your stuff
for the good of all the land of Egypt is yours
Pharaoh's bounty making the family's old goods not worth the carrying
a token of how God royally provides for his own

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: the wagons (Hebrew agaloth, from agal, round) were a sort of cart or wheeled vehicle, then little known; the witnesses note the sons of Israel doing as commanded, and Joseph giving them wagons by Pharaoh's order and provision for the way — the obedient brothers furnished with all needful for the journey (all provided for the return: 'the children of Israel did so: and Joseph gave them wagons, according to the commandment of Pharaoh, and gave them provision for the way'; the obedience, 'the children of Israel did so,' that the brothers did as Pharaoh and Joseph directed, making ready for the journey; that 'Joseph gave them wagons, according to the commandment of Pharaoh,' carrying out the king's order, providing the carts, a thing then little known, to bear the women, children, and aged father; the further care, 'and gave them provision for the way,' that Joseph furnished food for the long journey, that they should lack nothing; the full and thoughtful provision of Joseph, leaving nothing wanting for the bringing down of his father's house).

themes: the children of Israel did so: and Joseph gave them wagons, according to the commandment of Pharaoh
and gave them provision for the way
the obedient brothers furnished with wagons and provision for the journey
Joseph's full and thoughtful care, leaving nothing wanting

schools.summary: Gill: he gave them rich apparel, two suits of clothes to shift and change upon occasion, such as Homer calls 'changeable garments'; to Benjamin he gave three hundred pieces of silver and five changes; the witnesses note Joseph giving each brother changes of raiment, but to Benjamin three hundred pieces of silver and five changes — the parting gifts of love, with a marked bounty to Benjamin (Joseph sending them away enriched, his full brother most of all: 'To all of them he gave each man changes of raiment; but to Benjamin he gave three hundred pieces of silver, and five changes of raiment'; the gifts to all, 'each man changes of raiment,' rich apparel, fresh suits of clothes, a generous and honourable present to every brother; the special bounty to Benjamin, 'three hundred pieces of silver, and five changes of raiment,' a far greater gift to his only full brother, as at the feast he had given him the fivefold portion; that this open favour to Benjamin, far from stirring the old envy, was now received in love, the brothers changed and reconciled; the generosity of Joseph, who would not send his brethren away empty but laden with tokens of his love).

themes: To all of them he gave each man changes of raiment
but to Benjamin he gave three hundred pieces of silver, and five changes of raiment
Joseph's parting gifts of love, with a marked bounty to Benjamin
the open favour now received in love, the brothers changed and reconciled

schools.summary: Gill: he sent to his father after this manner, or according to this, in like manner as he gave his brethren, so he sent the like to him and more; the witnesses note Joseph sending his father ten asses laden with the good things of Egypt and ten she-asses laden with corn, bread, and meat for the way — the bountiful provision sent to honour and sustain the aged Jacob (Joseph loading gifts for his father: 'to his father he sent... ten asses laden with the good things of Egypt, and ten she asses laden with corn and bread and meat for his father by the way'; the richness of the present, 'ten asses laden with the good things of Egypt,' the choicest products of the land, sent to honour his father and to show him the plenty that awaited; the practical care, 'ten she asses laden with corn and bread and meat for his father by the way,' full provision of food for the journey, that Jacob and his house should not want on the road; the love of Joseph, lavishing on his father both honour and sustenance, the long-mourned son now able to provide royally for the old man; that the abundance was itself a proof of Joseph's greatness, fit to persuade Jacob of the wonderful news).

themes: to his father he sent... ten asses laden with the good things of Egypt
ten she asses laden with corn and bread and meat for his father by the way
Joseph's rich gift, lavishing honour and sustenance on the aged Jacob
the abundance itself a proof of his greatness, fit to persuade Jacob

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: this prudent caution, 'see that ye fall not out by the way,' lest they should mutually reproach each other for selling Joseph; the witnesses note Joseph sending his brethren away with the charge, 'See that ye fall not out by the way' — the wise and tender parting counsel, guarding against strife and recrimination on the road (Joseph, knowing their hearts, giving a word of peace: 'he sent his brethren away, and they departed: and he said unto them, See that ye fall not out by the way'; the prudence of the charge, that Joseph, knowing the guilt that lay on them, foresaw they might 'fall out' on the journey, each reproaching the others for the old sin of selling him, or quarrelling over who was most to blame; the tenderness of it, that, having forgiven them, Joseph would not have them torment one another with mutual blame but go in peace; the wider counsel the commentators draw, that those who are at peace with God and forgiven should live in peace among themselves, not raking up old wrongs; the wisdom of Joseph, caring for the harmony of the family he was gathering).

themes: So he sent his brethren away, and they departed
See that ye fall not out by the way
Joseph's wise parting word, guarding against strife and mutual blame
having forgiven them, he would have them go in peace, not torment one another

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: here is the good news brought to Jacob, the relation of it and the effect on the aged father; the witnesses note the brethren going up out of Egypt and coming into Canaan to their father Jacob — the homeward journey accomplished, the bearers of wonderful news drawing near to Jacob (the brothers returning with their tidings: 'they went up out of Egypt, and came into the land of Canaan unto Jacob their father'; the journey, 'they went up out of Egypt,' Canaan lying higher than Egypt, the brothers, now laden with gifts and provision, making their way home; the destination, 'unto Jacob their father,' the old man who had sent them in fear and who waited, all unknowing of the joy they brought; the weight of the moment, that the brothers carried news that would astonish and revive their father, that the son he had mourned as dead these twenty-two years was alive and lord of Egypt; the providence drawing all toward the gathering of the whole house to Joseph).

themes: And they went up out of Egypt
and came into the land of Canaan unto Jacob their father
the homeward journey, bearers of wonderful news to Jacob
that the son mourned as dead was alive and lord of Egypt

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: they told him, Joseph is yet alive, and he is governor over all Egypt, and his heart was numb, for he believed them not; the witnesses note the brethren telling Jacob that Joseph was yet alive and governor over Egypt, and Jacob's heart fainting, for he believed them not — the staggering news that overwhelmed the old man past believing (the tidings striking Jacob with a shock: 'Joseph is yet alive, and he is governor over all the land of Egypt. And Jacob's heart fainted, for he believed them not'; the greatness of the news, 'Joseph is yet alive, and he is governor over all the land of Egypt,' two wonders at once, that the son long mourned as dead was alive, and that he was raised to rule all Egypt; the effect, 'Jacob's heart fainted,' his heart grew numb or cold, was as if it stopped, that the news was so sudden and so great that the old man was overwhelmed, his heart failing him for very astonishment; that 'he believed them not,' that the tidings seemed too good to be true, and Jacob, who had so long given Joseph up for dead, could not at first take it in; that great and sudden joy may stun as well as great grief).

themes: Joseph is yet alive, and he is governor over all the land of Egypt
And Jacob's heart fainted, for he believed them not
the staggering news overwhelming the old man past believing
the tidings too good to be true, great joy stunning as well as great grief

schools.summary: Gill: they told him all the words of Joseph which he had said unto them, and the sight of the wagons confirmed the truth of the report; the witnesses note the brethren telling Jacob all Joseph's words, and the sight of the wagons reviving the spirit of their father — the gradual persuasion of Jacob, the words and the wagons together winning belief and reviving him (the proofs prevailing and the old man revived: 'they told him all the words of Joseph... and when he saw the wagons which Joseph had sent to carry him, the spirit of Jacob their father revived'; the means of conviction, first 'all the words of Joseph,' the full message of his love and his bidding to come down, which carried the ring of truth, and then, above all, 'the wagons which Joseph had sent to carry him,' the visible token that confirmed the report beyond doubt; the effect, 'the spirit of Jacob their father revived,' that his fainting heart was restored, his spirit, as it were dead within him for grief, came to life again at the proof that Joseph lived; the tenderness of the wagons sent 'to carry him,' a son's care that touched the old man's heart; how God restores the cast-down by sure tokens of his goodness).

themes: they told him all the words of Joseph, which he had said unto them
the spirit of Jacob their father revived
Jacob revived by Joseph's words and the sight of the wagons
the visible token confirming the report, his fainting heart restored

schools.summary: Gill: Israel said, it is enough, or it is much, it is great; he had much joy, as the Targums; this was the greatest blessing of all and more to him than all the rest; the witnesses note Israel saying, 'It is enough; Joseph my son is yet alive: I will go and see him before I die' — the old man's overflowing joy and his one desire, to see his living son once more (Jacob, now believing, content: 'It is enough; Joseph my son is yet alive: I will go and see him before I die'; the fulness of his joy, 'It is enough,' or 'it is much,' 'it is great,' that this one thing, that 'Joseph my son is yet alive,' was joy enough, blessing enough, the greatest of all blessings, outweighing every grief and want; the tender naming, 'Joseph my son,' the son so long mourned, now owned with a full heart; the one desire it kindled, 'I will go and see him before I die,' that Jacob asked nothing of Joseph's glory or wealth but only to see his face once more before his death, the longing of a father's love; the change from fainting unbelief to settled, restful joy, the old man's heart at peace; the providence bringing Jacob at last toward the reunion and the settling in Egypt).

themes: It is enough; Joseph my son is yet alive
I will go and see him before I die
Jacob's overflowing joy, this one thing blessing enough
his one desire, to see his living son's face once more before he died
